description: This figure presents mechanical and thermal characterization data investigating hydrogen embrittlement in Fe-26Mn-11Al-1.2C low-density austenitic steel strengthened by κ-carbides. The main panel displays engineering stress versus engineering plastic strain curves comparing specimens tested without hydrogen charging (black curve) and with hydrogen charging (red curve). The uncharged specimen exhibits high strength (~1000 MPa) and substantial ductility exceeding 45% plastic strain, whereas the hydrogen-charged specimen—subjected to 40 min precharging plus continuous in situ charging during testing—shows catastrophic embrittlement with failure occurring below 5% strain, indicating severe hydrogen-induced ductility loss. The inset shows thermal desorption analysis performed at a heating rate of 26 K/min, revealing multiple hydrogen desorption rate peaks (experiment, peak fit, and cumulative fit curves) that caption states correspond to hydrogen trapping at κ-carbide precipitates with an estimated activation energy of 76–80 kJ/mol determined via Redlich–Kister analysis. Together, these data demonstrate that while κ-carbides provide effective precipitation strengthening, they simultaneously act as potent hydrogen trap sites that promote embrittlement in this lightweight steel system.
